advanced-pet-diagnostics-pune.title: Advanced Pet Diagnostics in Pune — What Tests Your Pet Might Need
advanced-pet-diagnostics-pune.summary: Is your pet feeling unwell but the symptoms are vague? Learn about modern diagnostic tests at Shivraj Pet Clinic for dogs, cats, rabbits, birds, and turtles.
advanced-pet-diagnostics-pune.content:
Is your pet feeling unwell, but the symptoms are vague? That's where advanced veterinary diagnostics come in. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab in Pimple Nilakh, Pune, we offer a range of modern diagnostic tests that help us detect and treat diseases in dogs, cats, rabbits, birds, turtles, and other exotic pets — quickly and accurately.

Why Pet Diagnostics Matter

Animals can't tell us what's wrong. Early detection through diagnostics can prevent serious illness, help tailor precise treatments, and save lives in emergencies. We're equipped with in-clinic diagnostic tools, reducing the need for external referrals and speeding up treatment.

Key Diagnostic Services at Shivraj Pet Clinic

1. Digital X-Rays (Radiography)

Digital X-rays detect fractures, foreign bodies, tumours, pneumonia, bladder stones, and spinal issues. Non-invasive, fast, and pet-safe — they are routinely used for orthopedic and abdominal cases. Results are available within minutes at our clinic.

2. Ultrasound (USG)

Ultrasound is used for abdominal scans, pregnancy detection, and organ health monitoring — liver, kidney, spleen. Painless and safe for all species including birds and rabbits, it provides real-time images without any radiation.

3. Blood Tests — CBC, LFT, KFT

  • CBC (Complete Blood Count): Detects infections, anaemia, and immune response
  • LFT (Liver Function Test): Evaluates liver health through enzymes such as ALT, AST, ALP, and bilirubin. Especially useful for pets with vomiting, jaundice, or chronic illness
  • KFT (Kidney Function Test): Checks creatinine, urea, and electrolyte balance to detect kidney disease early

Available for dogs, cats, rabbits, and turtles. Results in 15–20 minutes at our in-house lab.

4. Urine and Stool Analysis

Essential for identifying UTIs, crystals, worms, and gut infections. Recommended when pets show frequent urination, diarrhoea, or blood in stools.

5. Exotic Pet Diagnostics

We tailor diagnostics for small mammals, reptiles, and birds with specialised sampling and sedation protocols where needed.

When Should You Book a Diagnostic Test?

  • Vomiting, diarrhoea, or appetite loss for more than 24 hours
  • Sudden weight loss or gain
  • Laboured breathing or persistent coughing
  • Behavioural changes or unusual lethargy
  • Post-trauma or accident
  • Annual health screenings (recommended for all pets over 7 years)

Benefits of In-Clinic Diagnostics at Shivraj

  • No long waits or referrals — everything under one roof
  • CBC and biochemistry results in 15–20 minutes
  • Better treatment accuracy — diagnosis drives targeted treatment
  • Full transparency with pet parents — we explain every result clearly

We are conveniently located in Pimple Nilakh, Pune, near Baner, Vishal Nagar, and Aundh. Call 077569 65169 to schedule a diagnostic appointment today.

care-of-pet-birds.title: Care of Pet Birds — Common Mistakes to Avoid
care-of-pet-birds.summary: Pet birds bring joy and intelligence into our homes — but caring for them properly requires more than a cage and seeds. Learn the top bird care mistakes to avoid from Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pune.
care-of-pet-birds.content:
Pet birds bring joy, colour, and intelligence into our homes — but caring for them properly requires more than just a cage and seeds. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab in Pimple Nilakh, Pune, we treat many birds suffering from entirely preventable issues. Here is how to ensure your feathered friends stay healthy and happy.

Essentials of Bird Care

1. Spacious and Enriched Cage

Birds need room to fly, not just perch. Cramped cages lead to stress, feather plucking, and behavioural problems. Enrich the cage with mirrors, natural perches of varying diameters, swings, and toys. Change toys regularly to prevent boredom. The cage should be placed away from kitchen fumes, draughts, and direct afternoon sunlight.

2. Proper Nutrition

Avoid seed-only diets — they are high in fat and nutritionally incomplete. A balanced bird diet includes fresh fruits, leafy vegetables, calcium supplements, and species-appropriate pellets. Budgies, cockatiels, and African greys each have different dietary requirements. Ask our vets for a species-specific diet chart during your next visit.

3. Clean Environment

Daily cleaning of the cage tray, water bowl, and feeding dishes is essential. Dust and accumulated droppings can trigger respiratory issues in birds, who have extremely sensitive respiratory systems. Wash perches weekly.

4. Fresh Water Daily

Change drinking water every single day. Stale water harbours bacteria that can cause crop infections and diarrhoea in birds.

Common Mistakes Bird Owners Make

Using Teflon (non-stick) cookware — The fumes released when non-stick pans overheat are immediately toxic to birds. Switch to stainless steel or cast iron cookware if you have a bird at home.

Neglecting flight time — Birds need supervised out-of-cage time daily for physical and mental stimulation. Without it, they develop obesity, feather plucking, and aggression.

Ignoring beak and nail overgrowth — Overgrown beaks prevent normal eating. Overgrown nails can catch on cage bars and cause injury. Both require professional trimming.

Self-medicating or using online remedies — Birds deteriorate extremely fast when unwell. A bird that looks slightly off today can be critically ill within 24 hours. Professional veterinary care, not home remedies, is always the right call.

Keeping a bird alone without social interaction — Birds are flock animals. Isolation causes depression and feather-destructive behaviour. Daily interaction or a companion bird is important.

When to Visit Shivraj Pet Clinic

Bring your bird in if you notice tail bobbing while breathing, fluffed feathers for extended periods, discharge from the nares or eyes, changes in dropping colour or consistency, or sudden weight loss. These are all signs that need same-day veterinary attention.

We offer exotic bird health checkups, beak and nail trimming, nutritional counselling, and treatment for feather plucking, infections, and injuries. Call 077569 65169 to book a bird consultation.

monsoon-pet-care-tips.title: Monsoon Pet Care Tips for Dogs and Cats in Pune
monsoon-pet-care-tips.summary: Monsoon brings ticks, fungal infections, and leptospirosis risks for Pune pets. Here are 8 essential monsoon care tips for dogs and cats from Dr. Pritesh Vidhate, Shivraj Pet Clinic.
monsoon-pet-care-tips.content:
Monsoon brings relief from Pune's scorching summer — but for pet parents, it also brings ticks, skin infections, leptospirosis, and thunder anxiety. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we see a sharp rise in tick fever, skin infections, and gastroenteritis cases every July to October. Here is how to keep your dog or cat healthy through the rains.

8 Essential Monsoon Pet Care Tips

1. Clean and Dry After Every Walk

After outdoor walks, wipe down your pet's paws, legs, and underbelly with a dry towel. Pay attention to the spaces between toe pads where moisture collects and causes fungal infections. Never let your pet sit with wet paws — dry thoroughly before allowing them indoors.

2. Daily Tick Check

Ticks thrive in warm, humid conditions — exactly what Pune's monsoon provides. Even indoor pets are at risk as ticks hitchhike indoors on shoes and clothing. After every walk, run your fingers through your pet's coat, particularly around the neck, ears, between toes, and the groin area. Remove ticks immediately using tweezers — grasp close to the skin, pull straight out without twisting.

Tick fever (Ehrlichiosis, Babesiosis) can become life-threatening within days. If your dog develops sudden lethargy, high fever, or stops eating, visit us immediately.

3. Keep Ears Clean and Dry

Moisture trapped in the ear canal is a leading cause of ear infections during monsoon. Clean your pet's ears weekly with a vet-recommended ear cleaner. Dogs with long, floppy ears (Labradors, Spaniels, Beagles) are especially vulnerable. Signs of ear infection include head shaking, scratching at ears, and a foul smell.

4. Avoid Puddles and Stagnant Water

Puddles and stagnant water contain Leptospira bacteria, which causes leptospirosis — a serious disease that affects both pets and humans. Keep dogs away from waterlogged areas, puddles, and floodwater during walks. Ensure fresh, clean drinking water is available at all times.

5. Maintain Vaccination and Deworming

Monsoon increases parasite load and infectious disease risk. Ensure your pet is up to date on all core vaccinations and deworming. Puppies and kittens should be on the monthly deworming schedule. Adult dogs and cats should be dewormed every three months.

6. Watch for Skin Infections

The combination of humidity and wet fur creates the perfect environment for bacterial and fungal skin infections. Signs include red patches, excessive scratching, hair loss, and a musty smell. Keep your pet's coat groomed and dry. Use an antifungal shampoo if recommended by your vet.

7. Manage Thunder Anxiety

Thunder and lightning cause genuine fear in many dogs. Create a safe, enclosed space — a covered crate with familiar bedding works well. Stay calm yourself; your pet mirrors your emotional state. Play soft music to mask the sound of thunder. Do not punish fearful behaviour.

8. Adjust Diet and Exercise

With reduced outdoor exercise during heavy rains, slightly reduce caloric intake to prevent weight gain. Increase indoor play and mental stimulation — puzzle feeders, hide-and-seek games, and training sessions are excellent during monsoon confinement.

If your pet develops vomiting, diarrhoea, lethargy, or fever during monsoon season, do not wait. Call 077569 65169 — we are open Mon–Tue and Thu–Sun at Pimple Nilakh.

neutering-rabbits-pune.title: Neutering Rabbits — Why It Matters and How We Do It Safely in Pune
neutering-rabbits-pune.summary: Rabbit neutering prevents reproductive cancers, reduces aggression, and extends lifespan. Learn how Shivraj Pet Clinic performs safe rabbit surgery in Pune using gaseous anaesthesia.
neutering-rabbits-pune.content:
Neutering refers to the surgical removal of reproductive organs — castration for males (bucks) and spaying for females (does). For rabbits, it is one of the most important health decisions you can make as a pet owner. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we have safely performed rabbit neutering for pet parents across Pimple Nilakh, Baner, Aundh, and Wakad.

Why Should You Neuter Your Rabbit?

Prevents reproductive cancers — Uterine cancer affects over 80% of unspayed female rabbits by age 5. Spaying completely eliminates this risk.

Reduces aggression and territorial behaviour — Unneutered rabbits often bite, lunge, grunt, and spray urine to mark territory. Neutering dramatically reduces these behaviours in both males and females.

Allows safe bonding between rabbits — A neutered male and spayed female can live together harmoniously without risk of pregnancy.

Improves hygiene — Neutered rabbits spray urine far less, making their living space cleaner and easier to maintain.

Longer, healthier life — Studies consistently show neutered rabbits live significantly longer than intact ones. Removal of reproductive organs eliminates associated diseases.

Common Myths About Rabbit Neutering

"They are too small for surgery" — Not true. We have safely neutered rabbits weighing as little as 1.2 kg. The key is appropriate anaesthesia and surgical technique, not size.

"It changes their personality" — Neutering actually makes rabbits calmer and more affectionate. Their core personality remains intact.

"Rabbits recover too slowly" — Rabbits typically return to normal activity within 48–72 hours when proper pain management and post-operative care protocols are followed.

How We Perform Rabbit Neutering at Shivraj Pet Clinic

  1. Pre-surgical examination and bloodwork — We assess your rabbit's health before anaesthesia
  2. Gaseous anaesthesia — Essential for small exotics; far safer than injectable-only anaesthesia
  3. Sterile surgical environment — Full surgical suite with appropriate monitoring
  4. Pain management — Post-operative analgesia for comfortable recovery
  5. Follow-up appointment within 7 days — To check healing and remove sutures if needed

When Is the Right Age?

Males can be castrated from 3.5 to 4 months of age, once the testicles have fully descended. Females are ideally spayed between 5 and 6 months. Surgery in older rabbits carries slightly higher risk due to fatty tissue around the reproductive organs, but is still routinely performed with appropriate pre-operative screening.

Call 077569 65169 to schedule rabbit spaying or castration at Shivraj Pet Clinic in Pimple Nilakh. We are one of the few clinics in PCMC performing exotic pet surgery with gaseous anaesthesia.

new-puppy-kitten-guide.title: New Puppy and Kitten Guide — First 6 Months Care Tips from Shivraj Pet Clinic
new-puppy-kitten-guide.summary: Just brought home a puppy or kitten? This complete guide covers vaccination schedule, deworming, feeding, socialisation, and first vet visit — from Dr. Pritesh Vidhate, Pimple Nilakh Pune.
new-puppy-kitten-guide.content:
Bringing home a new puppy or kitten is one of life's great joys — and one of its great responsibilities. The first six months are the most critical period for your pet's health, immunity, and behavioural development. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we guide hundreds of new pet parents through this journey every year.

Week 1: The First Vet Visit

Bring your new puppy or kitten to the clinic within the first week of bringing them home — even if they appear healthy. We will perform a complete physical examination, check for congenital issues, assess body condition, and create a personalised vaccination and deworming schedule.

Vaccination Schedule

Puppies:

  • 1.5–2 months: Puppy DP (Distemper, Parvovirus)
  • 2–2.5 months: DHPPiL 9-in-1
  • 3 months: 11-in-1 vaccine
  • 3 months: Anti-Rabies (mandatory by law)
  • 3 months+: Kennel Cough (especially for dogs that visit parks or grooming)
  • Annual boosters for all vaccines thereafter

Kittens:

  • 2 months: Tricat (Panleukopenia, Calicivirus, Rhinotracheitis)
  • 3 months: Anti-Rabies
  • Annual boosters

Do not take your puppy to public spaces, dog parks, or areas frequented by other dogs until at least two weeks after their second vaccine. Their immunity is not yet fully developed.

Deworming Schedule

Puppies and kittens are often born with internal parasites transmitted from the mother. Deworming schedule:

  • Every 15 days from birth to 3 months
  • Monthly from 3–6 months
  • Every 3 months for life thereafter

Never skip deworming — roundworms and hookworms from pets can infect humans, particularly children.

Feeding Your New Pet

Puppies: Feed 3–4 times daily up to 6 months, then twice daily. Choose a high-quality puppy food appropriate for the breed size. Large breed puppies need controlled calcium — do not supplement unless your vet recommends it.

Kittens: Feed 3–4 times daily using a kitten-specific formulation. Cats are obligate carnivores — do not feed vegetarian diets.

Always provide fresh water. Avoid cow's milk — both puppies and kittens are commonly lactose intolerant.

Socialisation — The Critical Window

Between 3 and 12 weeks, puppies are in the critical socialisation window. Expose them to different people, sounds, surfaces, and environments (after partial vaccination). Well-socialised puppies grow into confident, calm adult dogs. Kittens have a shorter socialisation window of 2–7 weeks.

Common First-Year Health Issues

  • Parvovirus — life-threatening diarrhoea in unvaccinated puppies. Prevention through vaccination is essential.
  • Distemper — neurological disease prevented by vaccination.
  • Panleukopenia in kittens — highly contagious, often fatal without early intervention.
  • Ringworm — fungal skin infection, transmissible to humans.
  • Ear mites — common in kittens, causes intense scratching.

Spaying and Neutering

We recommend spaying female dogs and cats before their first heat (around 5–6 months) to prevent uterine infections (pyometra) and mammary cancer. Male dogs and cats benefit from neutering at 5–6 months to reduce roaming, aggression, and reproductive cancers.

Call 077569 65169 to book your new puppy or kitten's first appointment at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh.

pre-post-operative-care-pets.title: Pre-operative & Post-operative Care for Pets — Surgery Safety at Shivraj
pre-post-operative-care-pets.summary: What to expect before and after your pet's surgery at Shivraj Pet Clinic. Our gas anaesthesia protocol, recovery monitoring and complete post-op care guide.
pre-post-operative-care-pets.content:
Our Commitment to Surgical Safety

At Shivraj Pet Clinic we have performed over 200 surgical procedures on exotic and companion animals. Our protocol prioritises safety at every step.

🏥 We use gas anaesthesia (isoflurane) for all surgeries — the same standard used in human hospitals. Significantly safer than injectable-only anaesthesia.

Pre-operative Preparation

Dogs and Cats:

  • Fast for 8–12 hours before surgery — no food after midnight
  • Water is fine up to 2 hours before
  • Pre-surgical blood work — we run CBC and biochemistry
  • Do NOT give medications on the morning of surgery unless instructed

Exotic Animals (Rabbits, Guinea Pigs, Birds):

  • Do NOT fast rabbits or guinea pigs — their digestive system must keep moving
⚠️ Most dangerous mistake: fasting a rabbit before surgery. Always tell us if another vet recommended this.

Post-operative Care at Home

  • Rest for 24–48 hours — no running, jumping or stairs
  • E-collar (cone) — must be worn to prevent licking the wound
  • Wound care: Keep dry, check daily for redness or discharge
  • Medications: Give exactly as prescribed — never skip pain relief
  • Follow-up: Suture removal 10–14 days after surgery

hip-dysplasia-dogs.title: Hip Dysplasia in Dogs — Causes, Symptoms, and Treatment Options in Pune
hip-dysplasia-dogs.summary: Hip dysplasia causes chronic pain and mobility problems in dogs. Learn the signs, breeds at risk, diagnosis, and treatment options available at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh Pune.
hip-dysplasia-dogs.content:
Hip dysplasia is one of the most common skeletal conditions in dogs, causing pain, reduced mobility, and significantly reduced quality of life. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we diagnose and manage hip dysplasia regularly — and earlier detection always leads to better outcomes.

What is Hip Dysplasia?

Hip dysplasia is a developmental condition where the ball and socket of the hip joint do not fit together properly. Instead of moving smoothly, the joint grinds and shifts, causing erosion of cartilage, inflammation, and over time, secondary arthritis. It is primarily a genetic condition, but diet, exercise, and growth rate also influence severity.

Breeds Most Commonly Affected

  • German Shepherds
  • Labrador Retrievers
  • Golden Retrievers
  • Rottweilers
  • Great Danes
  • Saint Bernards
  • Bulldogs

Smaller breeds can also be affected but less commonly.

Signs and Symptoms

Hip dysplasia can appear as early as 5–6 months in severe cases, or not until middle age in mild cases. Signs include:

  • Reluctance to rise from lying down, especially in the morning
  • Bunny-hopping gait — moving both hind legs together rather than alternating
  • Swaying or wobbling at the hindquarters during walking
  • Difficulty climbing stairs or jumping into cars
  • Hind leg muscle wasting (the hindquarters look thinner than the front)
  • Pain on hip manipulation during examination
  • Behavioural changes — reduced activity, reluctance to play

Diagnosis

Diagnosis requires physical examination and X-rays. We position your dog in a specific way for hip radiographs that allows assessment of the degree of joint laxity and arthritis present. Blood tests are performed to rule out other causes of lameness.

Treatment Options

Treatment depends on age, severity, and how much arthritis has developed.

Medical Management (conservative):

  • Weight management — reducing load on the joint is the single most effective intervention
  • Controlled, low-impact exercise — swimming and leash walks, not running
  • Pain medication (NSAIDs) — prescribed by your vet, never human ibuprofen or paracetamol
  • Joint supplements — glucosamine and chondroitin support cartilage health
  • Physiotherapy — targeted exercises to maintain muscle mass around the joint

Surgical Options: For younger dogs with no significant arthritis, surgical correction can provide long-term joint health. For older dogs with advanced arthritis, Total Hip Replacement (THR) or Femoral Head Ostectomy (FHO) are options. We discuss referral to orthopaedic specialists when surgical intervention is appropriate.

Prevention

While hip dysplasia is genetic, severity can be reduced by:

  • Keeping large breed puppies lean during the rapid growth phase (4–12 months)
  • Avoiding high-impact exercise on stairs and slippery floors during growth
  • Feeding a large-breed puppy food with controlled calcium

If your dog shows any signs of hind limb difficulty, do not wait. Call 077569 65169 — early management significantly slows arthritis progression.

exotic-pet-care-pune.title: Exotic Pet Care in Pune — Trusted Veterinary Support at Shivraj Pet Clinic
exotic-pet-care-pune.summary: Looking for a rabbit, bird, or reptile vet in Pune? Learn how Shivraj Pet Clinic offers expert care for exotic pets including parrots, turtles, hamsters, and more in Pimple Nilakh.
exotic-pet-care-pune.content:
Unlike dogs and cats, exotic pets have unique biology and subtle symptoms — making professional veterinary care absolutely essential. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we provide compassionate, species-specific care for birds, rabbits, turtles, reptiles, hamsters, and other small mammals.

Common Health Issues in Exotic Pets

Exotic pets often mask their discomfort. By the time signs appear, the issue can be serious. Here is what to watch for by species:

Birds: Egg binding, feather plucking, beak overgrowth. Signs include fluffed feathers, lethargy, and tail bobbing.

Rabbits: GI stasis, dental malocclusion, heat stroke. Watch for reduced appetite, drooling, and unusually quiet behaviour.

Turtles: Shell rot, Vitamin A deficiency. Signs include swollen eyes, soft shell, and nasal discharge.

Hamsters: Wet tail disease, overgrown teeth. Lethargy, diarrhoea, and weight loss are common indicators.

At Shivraj, we offer diagnostic imaging, blood testing, parasite screening, and fecal exams for all exotic species.

What We Offer for Exotic Pets

  • Health checkups tailored for each species
  • Gaseous anaesthesia — safe for birds, rabbits, and reptiles
  • In-house diagnostics for fast same-visit results
  • Surgery including abscess drainage and neutering in rabbits
  • Diet and husbandry counselling to prevent common diseases
  • Emergency care for exotic pets

We combine experience with patience — because treating a stressed guinea pig or a shy parrot needs a calm hand and the right approach.

Our Experience with Exotic Species

Dr. Pritesh Vidhate (M.V.Sc. – Medicine) has extensive clinical experience with exotic species. We are one of the few clinics in PCMC to perform safe neutering in rabbits, treat avian infections and injuries, manage reptile shell and metabolic disorders, and provide long-term dental care for rodents.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I bring my bird for beak or nail trimming? Yes. Regular grooming prevents injuries and overgrowth. We handle birds of all sizes — from budgies to African grey parrots.

Do you handle rabbit neutering safely? Yes. We use safe gaseous anaesthesia and tailored surgical protocols specifically for rabbits.

My turtle isn't eating — what should I do? Bring them in immediately. Appetite loss in reptiles can be a sign of serious internal problems including Vitamin A deficiency or respiratory infection.

Are exotic pets safe around children? Yes, with proper guidance. We offer handling tips and hygiene protocols to keep both pets and people safe.

Why Choose Shivraj Pet Clinic for Exotic Pets?

Trusted by pet parents from Pimple Nilakh, Baner, Aundh, and Wakad — we offer advanced diagnostics, surgical support, and species-specific care plans with follow-ups. Call 077569 65169 to schedule an appointment for your exotic pet.

tick-fever-dogs.title: Tick Fever in Dogs — Why Regular Testing is Important
tick-fever-dogs.summary: Tick fever (Ehrlichiosis and Babesiosis) is dangerously common in Pune's green neighbourhoods. Early blood testing is the only reliable way to catch it before it becomes fatal.
tick-fever-dogs.content:
What is Tick Fever?

Tick fever is a group of infections transmitted by tick bites. In Pune and PCMC we primarily see Ehrlichiosis and Babesiosis — both transmitted by the Brown Dog Tick, extremely common in green localities.

📍 High-risk areas in Pune: Pimple Nilakh, Wakad, Aundh, Baner, Pashan. If your dog goes outdoors, tick fever testing is essential twice a year.

Symptoms to Watch For

  • Sudden lethargy — energetic yesterday, barely moving today
  • Complete loss of appetite
  • High fever — above 103°F (39.4°C)
  • Pale or yellow gums — sign of anaemia
  • Blood in urine — dark brown or red (classic Babesiosis sign)
  • Bleeding from nose or gums — advanced Ehrlichiosis

Diagnosis and Treatment

We run CBC and tick fever panel in our in-house lab — results in 1–2 hours. Ehrlichiosis: Doxycycline for 4 weeks. Babesiosis: Imidocarb injections. Most dogs recover fully when caught early.

⚠️ Do not wait. Tick fever progresses from mild to critical in 48–72 hours. Call +91 77569 65169 immediately if your dog is lethargic and not eating.

Prevention

  • Monthly tick prevention — Bravecto, NexGard or Simparica
  • Check your dog for ticks after every outdoor walk
  • Annual tick fever blood test even without symptoms

dental-scaling-dogs-cats.title: Dental Scaling for Dogs and Cats — Why Your Pet Needs Regular Teeth Cleaning
dental-scaling-dogs-cats.summary: Over 80% of dogs develop dental disease by age 3. Learn why dental scaling is essential for your pet's health, what the procedure involves, and how to maintain oral hygiene at home.
dental-scaling-dogs-cats.content:
Over 80% of dogs and 70% of cats develop some form of dental disease by the age of 3 — yet dental health remains one of the most overlooked aspects of pet care. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we perform professional dental scaling and oral health assessments to prevent pain, tooth loss, and the serious systemic diseases that dental infection can cause.

Why Dental Health Matters

Dental disease is not just about bad breath. When bacteria accumulate in the mouth and form tartar (hardened calculus), they can enter the bloodstream and cause:

  • Heart valve disease — bacteria settling on heart valves
  • Kidney damage — chronic low-grade infection taxes kidney filtration
  • Liver disease — bacterial toxins processed by the liver
  • Chronic pain — loose, infected teeth cause constant discomfort that pets hide

Dogs and cats do not show pain the way humans do. A pet with severe dental disease often continues eating — right up until they cannot.

Signs Your Pet Needs Dental Attention

  • Bad breath (beyond normal "dog breath")
  • Yellow or brown deposits on teeth near the gum line
  • Red, swollen, or bleeding gums
  • Loose teeth
  • Drooling more than usual
  • Pawing at the mouth
  • Preference for soft food or dropping food while eating
  • Facial swelling under the eye (often indicates a tooth root abscess)

The Dental Scaling Procedure

Professional dental scaling at Shivraj Pet Clinic involves:

  1. Pre-anaesthetic bloodwork — essential before general anaesthesia
  2. General anaesthesia — pets must be fully anaesthetised for thorough and safe scaling. "Anaesthesia-free dentistry" is ineffective and stressful — a conscious pet cannot hold still for sub-gingival (below gum line) cleaning where disease lives.
  3. Scaling — ultrasonic and hand instruments remove tartar from all tooth surfaces
  4. Probing — checking for periodontal pockets and loose teeth
  5. Extraction — severely diseased teeth are extracted rather than left to cause ongoing pain and infection
  6. Polishing — smooths enamel to slow future tartar accumulation

Maintaining Oral Health at Home

  • Tooth brushing — daily brushing with a pet-safe toothpaste is the gold standard. Start slowly, let your pet lick the toothpaste first, introduce the brush gradually.
  • Dental chews — those with the VOHC (Veterinary Oral Health Council) seal have evidence behind them
  • Dental diets — certain prescription diets help reduce tartar mechanically
  • Water additives — some products reduce bacterial counts in drinking water

Professional dental scaling is typically needed every 1–3 years depending on your pet's breed and oral hygiene routine. Small breeds (Shih Tzu, Dachshund, Pomeranians) tend to develop tartar faster and may need annual scaling.

Call 077569 65169 to schedule a dental assessment for your pet at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh.

dangerous-human-medicines-dogs-cats.title: Dangerous Human Medicines for Dogs and Cats — What Never to Give Your Pet
dangerous-human-medicines-dogs-cats.summary: Common human painkillers and medicines that are safe for people can be fatal to pets. Dr. Pritesh Vidhate from Shivraj Pet Clinic explains which medicines to keep away from dogs and cats.
dangerous-human-medicines-dogs-cats.content:
One of the most common causes of pet poisoning that we see at Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab is well-intentioned owners giving human medicines to their pets. What is safe — or even therapeutic — in humans can be fatal in dogs and cats. Never give your pet any human medication without first consulting your veterinarian.

Most Dangerous Human Medicines for Dogs and Cats

1. Paracetamol (Crocin, Dolo, Tylenol)

Extremely toxic to cats. Dangerous to dogs in any dose.

Cats completely lack the liver enzyme (UGT1A6) needed to metabolise paracetamol. Even a single tablet can cause fatal liver failure and haemoglobin destruction in cats. Dogs can also suffer severe liver damage. There is no safe dose — never give paracetamol to any pet.

Signs of paracetamol toxicity: facial swelling, brown/blue gums (methaemoglobinaemia), laboured breathing, jaundice, collapse.

2. Ibuprofen and Diclofenac (Brufen, Combiflam, Voveran)

Toxic to both dogs and cats.

NSAIDs like ibuprofen cause gastrointestinal ulceration, kidney failure, and neurological signs in pets. Even one tablet of ibuprofen can cause severe gastric ulcers and kidney damage in a medium-sized dog. Diclofenac has caused mass deaths in vultures in India and is similarly dangerous to companion animals.

3. Aspirin

Aspirin is occasionally used in dogs at very specific doses under veterinary supervision — but even here, errors can be fatal. Cats cannot metabolise aspirin at all. A single aspirin tablet every 48 hours can accumulate to toxic levels in cats. Never use aspirin without explicit veterinary direction and dosing.

4. Antidepressants and Sleep Aids

Human antidepressants (SSRIs, tricyclics) and sedatives cause serious neurological toxicity in pets — tremors, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias, and coma. These are among the most common causes of accidental pet poisoning.

5. Xylitol (in sugar-free products and some medicines)

Xylitol is a sugar substitute found in sugar-free gum, some peanut butter brands, vitamin gummies, and oral care products. In dogs, it causes a rapid, severe drop in blood sugar (hypoglycaemia) and liver failure. Even small amounts — the equivalent of a few pieces of sugar-free gum — can be fatal to a small dog.

6. Vitamins and Supplements

Vitamin D (cholecalciferol) in excess is a common rat poison ingredient and causes fatal hypercalcaemia in pets. Vitamin C in high doses causes gastrointestinal upset. Iron supplements cause gastrointestinal haemorrhage and liver damage.

What to Do in Case of Accidental Ingestion

If your pet has consumed any human medication:

  1. Do not wait for symptoms to appear
  2. Call us immediately at 077569 65169
  3. Note the exact medication name, dose/tablet strength, and approximately how much was consumed
  4. Bring the packaging with you

Early treatment — within the first 1–2 hours — dramatically improves outcomes for most toxicities. Do not induce vomiting at home without veterinary guidance, as this can sometimes cause additional harm.

Shivraj Pet Clinic is open Mon–Tue and Thu–Sun at Pimple Nilakh. For emergency guidance, call 077569 65169.

high-rise-syndrome-cats.title: High Rise Syndrome in Cats — Causes, Dangers & Every Pet Owner Must Know
high-rise-syndrome-cats.summary: High Rise Syndrome is a growing danger for cats in Pune's apartments. Learn what injuries occur, exactly what to do immediately, and how to prevent it.
high-rise-syndrome-cats.content:
What is High Rise Syndrome?

High Rise Syndrome is the pattern of injuries when cats fall from apartment balconies. In Pune's high-rise neighbourhoods — Wakad, Pimple Nilakh, Baner, Aundh — we see multiple cases every month.

⚠️ The paradox: cats falling from 2–6 floors often sustain MORE injuries than from 7+ floors. Above terminal velocity cats relax and spread impact. Shorter falls can be deadlier.

Common Injuries

  • Fractured jaw — the chin hits first
  • Pneumothorax — collapsed lung from chest impact. Needs immediate treatment
  • Fractured limbs and pelvis
  • Internal organ damage — liver, spleen, bladder rupture
  • Spinal injuries — paralysis risk

What to Do Immediately

  1. Stay calm — panic worsens the cat's shock
  2. Do NOT cradle — possible spinal injury
  3. Slide cat onto a flat board or box — support whole body
  4. Keep warm — shock causes temperature drop
  5. Call us immediately: +91 77569 65169
⚠️ Never wait and watch. A cat that walks away may still have internal bleeding. Always get a post-fall examination. We have in-house X-ray and ultrasound for immediate diagnosis.

Prevention

Install balcony safety netting — ₹1,500–₹4,000 for a standard 2BHK balcony. This single investment prevents years of trauma.

toxic-foods-dogs-cats.title: Toxic Foods for Dogs and Cats — What Never to Feed Your Pet
toxic-foods-dogs-cats.summary: Grapes, onions, chocolate, and xylitol are just some of the common foods that can kill pets. Learn what foods are toxic to dogs and cats from Dr. Pritesh Vidhate, Shivraj Pet Clinic Pune.
toxic-foods-dogs-cats.content:
Many foods that humans eat daily are toxic — sometimes fatally — to dogs and cats. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, food toxicity is one of the most common emergency presentations we see. This guide covers the most dangerous foods and what to do if your pet eats them.

Foods Toxic to Dogs and Cats

Grapes and Raisins

Toxic to dogs — potentially toxic to cats.

Even small quantities of grapes or raisins can cause acute kidney failure in dogs. The toxic compound is not yet identified, meaning there is no safe amount. Some dogs eat a single grape and develop kidney failure; others eat a handful with no apparent effect. Because of this unpredictability, zero tolerance is the only safe approach. Do not share any grape-containing food — including juice, wine gums, or raisins in baked goods.

Onions, Garlic, and Leeks

Toxic to both dogs and cats. More dangerous to cats.

The entire allium family — onions, garlic, leeks, chives, and shallots — contains compounds that damage red blood cells, causing haemolytic anaemia. Toxicity occurs from raw, cooked, or powdered forms. The effect is cumulative — daily small amounts can build up over weeks. Indian home-cooked food shared with pets is a common source of this poisoning.

Signs: lethargy, pale gums, increased respiratory rate, orange or brown urine.

Chocolate

Toxic to dogs. Less common in cats who typically avoid sweet tastes.

Chocolate contains theobromine and caffeine, both toxic to dogs. Dark chocolate and baking chocolate are far more dangerous than milk chocolate, but no form is safe. A medium-sized dog eating a small bar of dark chocolate can develop serious toxicity.

Signs: vomiting, diarrhoea, hyperactivity, muscle tremors, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias.

Xylitol

Extremely toxic to dogs.

Xylitol is a sugar substitute found in sugar-free gum, some peanut butters, vitamin gummies, and dental products. In dogs, it causes a sudden severe drop in blood sugar (hypoglycaemia) and liver failure. Even one or two pieces of xylitol-containing gum can be fatal to a small dog.

Macadamia Nuts

Toxic to dogs.

Macadamia nuts cause weakness, hyperthermia, vomiting, and tremors in dogs, typically within 12 hours of ingestion.

Avocado

Toxic to birds and rabbits. Potentially harmful to dogs and cats in large amounts.

Persin, found in avocado flesh, skin, pit, and leaves, causes respiratory distress and fluid accumulation around the heart in birds. Keep avocado away from all pets.

Alcohol

Toxic to all pets.

Even small amounts of alcohol cause significant toxicity in pets — vomiting, disorientation, low blood sugar, low body temperature, and respiratory depression. Beer, wine, spirits, and even alcohol-containing desserts must be kept away from pets.

Raw Dough and Yeast

Toxic to dogs.

Raw yeast dough continues to expand in the warm, moist environment of the stomach, causing life-threatening bloat (gastric dilatation). Fermenting yeast also produces alcohol — creating a double toxicity.

What to Do If Your Pet Eats Something Toxic

  1. Do not wait for symptoms — call us immediately at 077569 65169
  2. Note exactly what was eaten, how much, and when
  3. Bring packaging or a photo of the product
  4. Do not attempt to induce vomiting at home without veterinary guidance

The window for effective treatment is often very narrow. If caught within 1–2 hours of ingestion, we can often prevent serious toxicity from developing. After symptoms appear, treatment becomes more complex and outcomes less certain.

Shivraj Pet Clinic is at Pimple Nilakh, Pune. Open Mon–Tue and Thu–Sun.

noise-phobia-pets.title: Noise Phobia in Pets — Managing Thunder and Firecracker Anxiety in Dogs and Cats
noise-phobia-pets.summary: Diwali firecrackers and monsoon thunder cause real distress in pets. Learn how to recognise and manage noise phobia in dogs and cats from Dr. Pritesh Vidhate, Shivraj Pet Clinic Pune.
noise-phobia-pets.content:
Noise phobia is a genuine psychological disorder in pets — not stubbornness or disobedience. Thunder, Diwali firecrackers, construction noise, and vehicle backfires all trigger extreme fear responses in dogs and cats. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we help pet parents in Pimple Nilakh, Baner, and surrounding areas manage noise phobia effectively.

Why Noise Phobia Happens

Dogs and cats have significantly more sensitive hearing than humans. What sounds loud to us is overwhelming to them. Additionally, dogs can sense changes in barometric pressure and static electricity before a storm even begins — meaning their anxiety often starts before the first crack of thunder.

Signs of Noise Phobia

In dogs:

  • Trembling, shaking, or panting excessively
  • Attempting to hide under furniture or in closets
  • Destructive behaviour — scratching at doors, chewing walls, destroying objects
  • Loss of bladder or bowel control
  • Inability to settle despite reassurance
  • Vocalization — howling, barking, whining
  • Attempting to escape — can lead to pets running into traffic

In cats:

  • Hiding under beds or in enclosed spaces
  • Refusing to eat or drink
  • Dilated pupils and flattened ears
  • Excessive grooming or grooming-related hair loss
  • Eliminating outside the litter box

Management Strategies

Environmental Changes

Create a dedicated safe space — a quiet, dark room away from windows. For dogs, a covered crate with familiar bedding can provide security. Close windows and curtains to muffle noise and block the visual stimuli of lightning.

Behavioural Approaches

Do not punish fearful behaviour — this worsens anxiety. Calm, confident presence from you is helpful. Do not over-reassure either — excessive coddling can reinforce the fear response. Desensitisation through recorded storm sounds at low volume, very gradually increased over weeks, can help in mild cases.

Calming Aids

  • Pressure wraps (anxiety vests) — provide gentle, constant pressure similar to swaddling. Effective in 30–40% of dogs.
  • Pheromone diffusers — Adaptil for dogs, Feliway for cats — synthetic pheromones that signal safety.
  • Calming supplements — certain amino acid and herbal combinations have evidence behind them.

Veterinary Medication

For moderate to severe noise phobia, medication is often necessary and humane. We prescribe appropriate anxiolytic medications depending on your pet's size, age, and health status. These are given before anticipated noise events (Diwali, New Year's Eve, monsoon season).

Do not give human sedatives (diazepam, alprazolam) without veterinary prescription and guidance. Incorrect dosing can cause respiratory depression and paradoxical agitation.

Preparing for Diwali

Start planning two weeks before Diwali. Talk to us about appropriate medication at your next visit. Keep pets indoors with music playing to mask outdoor sounds. Ensure all identification tags and microchipping are up to date — Diwali is the peak season for lost pets in India.

Call 077569 65169 to discuss noise phobia management for your pet. We are open Mon–Tue and Thu–Sun at Pimple Nilakh.

gastroenteritis-dogs-vaccination.title: Gastroenteritis in Dogs — Why Early Vaccination Can Save Your Puppy's Life
gastroenteritis-dogs-vaccination.summary: Parvovirus kills hundreds of puppies in Pune every year. Early vaccination is the only prevention. Here is our complete vaccination protocol and what to watch for.
gastroenteritis-dogs-vaccination.content:
What is Canine Gastroenteritis?

The most dangerous cause is Canine Parvovirus (CPV) — a highly contagious viral disease that kills rapidly, especially in unvaccinated puppies.

⚠️ Parvovirus survives in soil for up to 1 year. A puppy does not need to meet an infected dog to catch it — contaminated shoes, hands or grass are enough.

Signs of Parvovirus

  • Severe, bloody diarrhoea — often the first visible sign
  • Profuse vomiting — cannot keep water down
  • Extreme lethargy — puppy cannot stand or lift head
  • Complete loss of appetite

Vaccination Protocol

  • 6 weeks: DHPPi — Distemper, Hepatitis, Parvovirus, Parainfluenza
  • 9 weeks: DHPPi + Leptospirosis booster
  • 12 weeks: DHPPi + Rabies
  • 16 weeks: Final puppy booster + Kennel Cough
  • Annual: Booster + Rabies
⚠️ Do not take your unvaccinated puppy to dog parks, pet shops or public areas. Until the 12-week vaccine is complete, carry your puppy — do not let it walk on public ground.

Treatment if Infected

Intensive supportive care — IV fluids, anti-nausea medication, antibiotics. Survival with early treatment: 80–95%. Without treatment: less than 10%.

home-cooked-food-for-dogs.title: Home Cooked Food for Dogs — What Is Safe, What to Avoid, and How to Balance It
home-cooked-food-for-dogs.summary: Home cooked food is popular among Indian pet parents, but unbalanced diets cause nutritional deficiencies over time. Learn what to include, what to avoid, and how to balance home cooking for dogs.
home-cooked-food-for-dogs.content:
Home-cooked food is a common choice for Indian pet parents, and with good reason — you control the ingredients and can avoid preservatives. But unbalanced home diets are one of the most common causes of nutritional deficiency we see at Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab. A dog fed only rice and chicken for years without supplementation will develop bone disease, skin problems, and immune dysfunction.

What Dogs Need

Dogs are omnivores requiring a balance of:

  • Protein — the most critical macronutrient for muscle, immune function, and coat health
  • Carbohydrates — an energy source, though not strictly essential
  • Healthy fats — for skin, coat, and fat-soluble vitamin absorption
  • Vitamins and minerals — particularly calcium, phosphorus, vitamin D, and B vitamins
  • Fibre — for digestive health

The biggest mistake in home cooking is providing adequate protein and calories while neglecting minerals — particularly the calcium:phosphorus ratio, which is critical for bone development and maintenance.

Safe Foods to Include

Proteins: Chicken (boneless, cooked), lamb, eggs, fish (boneless, cooked), paneer in small amounts.

Vegetables: Carrots, green beans, peas, spinach, pumpkin, sweet potato (cooked). Start with small quantities to assess tolerance.

Grains: Plain cooked rice, oats, and millets are generally well tolerated.

Fruits (in moderation): Banana, apple (no seeds), watermelon (no rind or seeds), blueberries.

Foods to Strictly Avoid

  • Onions and garlic — haemolytic anaemia (toxic even in small amounts, including cooked forms)
  • Grapes and raisins — acute kidney failure
  • Bones — cooked bones splinter and cause intestinal perforations. Raw bones carry Salmonella risk.
  • Salt — Indian home food is too salty for dogs. Cook dog food separately with minimal to no salt.
  • Spices — chilli, turmeric in excess, pepper — irritate the gastrointestinal tract
  • Maida (refined flour) — nutritionally empty and promotes obesity
  • Dal and legumes in excess — high in lectins and phytates that interfere with mineral absorption

The Calcium Problem

A common homemade diet of rice, chicken, and vegetables is severely deficient in calcium. Over months, this leads to metabolic bone disease — especially dangerous in growing puppies and large breeds. If feeding home food, you must either add a calcium source (eggshell powder at 1⁄2 teaspoon per pound of food, or a veterinary calcium supplement) or have your vet calculate the appropriate supplementation.

Getting the Balance Right

True nutritional balance in home cooking requires calculation — it is more complex than it appears. We recommend:

  1. A consultation with our team to design a balanced recipe for your dog's size, age, and activity level
  2. Annual blood work to catch any nutritional deficiencies early
  3. Adding a veterinary multivitamin and mineral supplement to home-cooked diets

Many pet parents combine a good quality commercial food (50–70%) with home-cooked meals (30–50%) as a practical middle ground that ensures baseline nutritional coverage.

Call 077569 65169 to book a nutritional consultation at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh.

is-my-dog-cat-in-heat.title: Is My Dog or Cat in Heat? Signs, Symptoms, and What to Do
is-my-dog-cat-in-heat.summary: Recognising the signs of heat (oestrus) in dogs and cats helps prevent unwanted pregnancies and health issues. Learn what to expect and when to consider spaying or neutering.
is-my-dog-cat-in-heat.content:
Understanding your pet's reproductive cycle is important whether you plan to breed or not. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, many of the pyometra (infected uterus) emergencies we treat could have been prevented with earlier spaying. Here is what you need to know about heat in dogs and cats.

Dogs — Signs of Heat (Oestrus)

Female dogs (bitches) first come into heat between 6 and 24 months, depending on breed size. Small breeds mature earlier; giant breeds can take up to 18–24 months. After the first heat, cycles typically occur every 6–8 months.

Signs your dog is in heat:

  • Swollen vulva — the vulva enlarges noticeably
  • Bloody vaginal discharge — ranges from bright red to pink or straw-coloured as the cycle progresses
  • Increased urination — she urinates more frequently and in smaller amounts to spread her scent
  • Behavioural changes — increased restlessness, attention-seeking, or agitation
  • "Flagging" — when touched near the tail, she raises and deflects her tail to one side (indicates she is ready to mate)
  • Attracting male dogs — intact males may gather near your home

The heat cycle lasts approximately 21 days. The fertile period (when pregnancy can occur) is typically days 9–14, but this varies. Dogs can become pregnant during their very first heat.

Cats — Signs of Heat (Oestrus)

Cats are seasonally polyoestrous — they cycle repeatedly from January to October in India, coming into heat every 2–3 weeks if not mated. First heat typically occurs at 5–9 months.

Signs your cat is in heat:

  • Extreme vocalisation — loud, persistent yowling, especially at night, often mistaken by owners for pain
  • Rolling on the floor — rubbing her head and body along the floor and furniture
  • Assuming the mating posture — lordosis, crouching with the hindquarters raised and tail deflected
  • Restlessness and attempting to escape outdoors — a cat in heat will actively try to get outside to find a mate
  • Increased affection — unusually clingy with owners
  • Reduced appetite

Unlike dogs, cats in heat do not typically show vaginal bleeding.

What to Do When Your Pet is in Heat

  • Keep dogs on a lead at all times — an intact male dog can detect a female in heat from over a mile away
  • Keep cats strictly indoors — a determined male cat will find a way in, and a determined female in heat will find a way out
  • Do not use contraceptive injections repeatedly — progesterone-based injections to suppress heat significantly increase the risk of pyometra and mammary tumours

The Case for Spaying

We strongly recommend spaying female dogs before their first heat, and female cats by 5–6 months. Spaying before the first heat reduces mammary cancer risk by over 99%. It eliminates the risk of pyometra (infected uterus), which is a life-threatening emergency requiring surgery. It removes the management burden of repeated heat cycles.

Call 077569 65169 to schedule a spaying consultation at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh.

environmental-allergens-atopy-dogs-cats.title: Environmental Allergens That Trigger Atopy in Dogs and Cats
environmental-allergens-atopy-dogs-cats.summary: Atopic dermatitis (environmental allergy) is the second most common cause of itching in dogs. Learn which allergens trigger it, how it is diagnosed, and treatment options from Shivraj Pet Clinic Pune.
environmental-allergens-atopy-dogs-cats.content:
Atopic dermatitis — allergy triggered by environmental substances — is the second most common cause of chronic itching and skin disease in dogs, after flea allergy dermatitis. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we diagnose and manage atopy regularly. Understanding what triggers it helps you manage your pet's environment and reduce flare-ups.

What is Atopy?

Atopy (atopic dermatitis) is an inherited predisposition to develop allergic reactions to environmental substances that are inhaled or absorbed through the skin. Unlike food allergies (which cause gastrointestinal signs more often), atopy primarily causes skin disease — itching, redness, recurring ear infections, and skin fold infections.

Common Environmental Triggers

Dust mites (Dermatophagoides farinae and D. pteronyssinus) — the most common atopic allergen worldwide. Dust mites live in bedding, carpets, and upholstered furniture. They peak indoors year-round but worsen in humid monsoon conditions.

Moulds and fungi — Alternaria, Aspergillus, Penicillium, and Cladosporium spores are present outdoors and indoors, particularly in damp homes and during monsoon season.

Pollen — tree pollens (March–May), grass pollens (June–August), and weed pollens (September–November) cause seasonal atopy. Unlike humans who sneeze, dogs with pollen allergy typically itch.

Insect proteins — storage mites in food bags, cockroach allergens, and insect fragments.

Human dander — yes, some pets are allergic to humans.

Breeds Predisposed to Atopy

  • German Shepherds
  • Labradors and Golden Retrievers
  • Bulldogs and French Bulldogs
  • Boxers
  • West Highland White Terriers
  • Shih Tzus and Lhasa Apsos
  • Cocker Spaniels

Signs of Atopy in Dogs

  • Constant licking of paws — the feet become reddish-brown from saliva staining
  • Rubbing the face on furniture or carpet
  • Scratching at armpits, groin, and belly
  • Recurring ear infections — often the first sign
  • Red, inflamed skin between the toes
  • Secondary skin infections (bacterial and yeast) from chronic scratching
  • Worsening symptoms during specific seasons (if pollen-triggered) or year-round (if dust mite-triggered)

Signs of Atopy in Cats

Cats with atopy typically develop miliary dermatitis (tiny crusty bumps), eosinophilic plaques, and symmetrical hair loss from excessive grooming. Head and neck itching is common.

Diagnosis

Atopy is a clinical diagnosis supported by ruling out other causes of itching — particularly flea allergy and food allergy. We may recommend:

  • A strict flea prevention trial (fleas cause identical signs and must be eliminated first)
  • A dietary elimination trial to rule out food allergy
  • Intradermal allergy testing or serum allergy testing to identify specific triggers

Treatment Options

Environmental control — reduce dust mite load by washing bedding in hot water weekly, using allergen-proof mattress covers, and running a HEPA air purifier. Avoid synthetic carpets and soft furnishings where mites accumulate.

Medicated shampoos — antifungal and antiseborrhoeic shampoos reduce secondary infections and remove allergens from the skin surface.

Antihistamines — helpful in mild cases but only effective in about 30% of atopic dogs.

Fatty acid supplementation — omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids improve the skin barrier and reduce inflammation.

Immunotherapy (allergy shots or sublingual drops) — the only treatment that addresses the underlying immune dysregulation rather than suppressing symptoms. Effective in 60–80% of atopic patients when tailored to specific allergens.

Targeted medications (Apoquel, Cytopoint) — highly effective at controlling itch with good safety profiles. Available at our clinic.

Call 077569 65169 to schedule an allergy assessment at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh, Pune.

food-allergies-dogs.title: Common Food Allergies & Allergens in Dogs — Complete Guide
food-allergies-dogs.summary: Food allergies in dogs are widely underdiagnosed. Learn the signs, most common triggers, how to diagnose properly, and what to feed a dog with allergies.
food-allergies-dogs.content:
Are Food Allergies Common in Dogs?

Yes — and widely underdiagnosed. Food allergies account for 10–15% of all allergy cases we see. Symptoms overlap with environmental allergies, making diagnosis a process of careful elimination.

🔍 Key clue: Environmental allergies are seasonal. Food allergies happen year-round. If your dog itches in winter — think food first.

Signs of a Food Allergy

  • Itching — especially paws, ears, groin, and belly
  • Recurrent ear infections — diet is often the cause
  • Chronic skin issues — red, flaky, irritated skin year-round
  • Digestive issues — loose stools, vomiting, excessive gas

Most Common Allergens

  1. Chicken — most common in Indian dogs
  2. Beef, Dairy, Wheat, Eggs, Soy

Diagnosis

The gold standard is an elimination diet trial — 8–12 weeks feeding a novel protein your dog has never eaten. Blood tests and skin prick tests for food allergies in dogs are unreliable.

⚠️ Do not rely on quick test kits sold online. Only the elimination diet trial gives a definitive answer.

prp-injection-joint-diseases-dogs.title: Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) Injection for Joint Disease in Dogs
prp-injection-joint-diseases-dogs.summary: PRP therapy uses your dog's own blood to promote joint healing and reduce arthritis pain. Learn how platelet-rich plasma injections work, which conditions benefit, and what to expect.
prp-injection-joint-diseases-dogs.content:
Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) therapy is a regenerative medicine technique that harnesses the healing factors within your dog's own blood to reduce joint inflammation, slow cartilage degradation, and improve mobility. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, we discuss PRP as part of comprehensive joint disease management for appropriate patients.

What is PRP?

Platelet-Rich Plasma is a concentrated preparation of platelets derived from your dog's own blood. Platelets are best known for their role in clotting, but they also release growth factors — chemical signals that promote tissue healing, reduce inflammation, and stimulate the production of new collagen and cartilage matrix.

In the context of joint disease, injecting PRP directly into an affected joint delivers a concentrated dose of these healing signals where they are needed most.

How PRP is Prepared

  1. A blood sample is taken from your dog (typically 10–20ml)
  2. The blood is placed in a centrifuge and spun at specific speeds to separate platelets from red blood cells and other components
  3. The platelet-concentrated fraction is collected — this is the PRP
  4. Under sedation, the PRP is injected directly into the affected joint using sterile technique
  5. The entire process takes approximately 30–45 minutes

Which Joint Conditions Benefit from PRP?

PRP is most commonly used for:

  • Osteoarthritis (OA) — the most common indication. PRP reduces inflammation and may slow cartilage loss over time.
  • Hip dysplasia — as an adjunct to medical management in dogs not yet requiring surgery
  • Elbow dysplasia — early to moderate disease
  • Cruciate ligament injury — some evidence supports PRP as part of post-surgical recovery
  • Shoulder joint disease — osteochondrosis dissecans and bicipital tenosynovitis

PRP is generally most effective in mild to moderate joint disease. In advanced osteoarthritis with severe cartilage loss, PRP provides less benefit but can still reduce pain.

Benefits of PRP

  • Autologous — uses the patient's own blood, eliminating the risk of immune reaction or disease transmission
  • Reduces reliance on long-term NSAIDs — important for senior dogs with kidney or gastrointestinal concerns
  • Minimal downtime — most dogs resume normal activity within 48–72 hours
  • Repeatable — can be performed every 4–6 months as needed

What to Expect After Treatment

Mild soreness at the injection site for 2–3 days is normal — this is part of the initial inflammatory response that precedes healing. Most owners report noticeable improvement in mobility and comfort within 3–4 weeks. Some dogs require 2–3 treatments for optimal response.

PRP is most effective as part of a comprehensive arthritis management programme that includes weight control, appropriate exercise, and physiotherapy.

To discuss whether PRP therapy is appropriate for your dog's joint condition, call 077569 65169 and book a consultation at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh.

complete-diet-guide-budgies-cockatiels-p.title: Complete Diet Guide for Budgies, Cockatiels, and Parrots
complete-diet-guide-budgies-cockatiels-p.summary: Seed-only diets are the leading cause of nutritional disease in pet birds. Learn the ideal diet for budgies, cockatiels, African grey parrots, and macaws — from Shivraj Pet Clinic Pune.
complete-diet-guide-budgies-cockatiels-p.content:
Nutritional disease is the most common preventable cause of illness in pet birds. At Shivraj Pet Clinic & Lab, the majority of sick birds we see have some component of dietary deficiency — most often from a seed-only diet fed with the best intentions. Here is the complete guide to feeding your bird properly.

Why Seed-Only Diets Fail

Seeds are high in fat and severely deficient in vitamins A, D3, calcium, and many essential amino acids. A bird fed seeds exclusively will appear healthy for years — then begin to show signs of nutritional disease: feather quality deterioration, respiratory problems, immune deficiency, and reproductive failure. By the time clinical signs appear, deficiency has often been present for months.

The Ideal Bird Diet — By Species

Budgerigars (Budgies)

Pellets: 50–60% of the diet. A quality small pellet (Harrison's, Zupreem) provides complete baseline nutrition.

Fresh vegetables: 20–30%. Leafy greens (spinach, coriander, methi), carrot, cucumber, and broccoli. Offer daily.

Seeds: 10–15% maximum. Millets and canary grass seed are preferred over sunflower and safflower.

Fruits: 5–10%. Small amounts of apple (no seeds), papaya, mango, and guava. Remove uneaten fresh food within 4 hours.

Avoid: Avocado, onion, garlic, chocolate, caffeine, alcohol, and any pitted fruit seeds.

Cockatiels

Cockatiels benefit from a similar diet to budgies but with slightly more variety and larger portions. They particularly enjoy fresh corn, sweet potato, and small cooked legumes. Cuttlebone should always be available as a calcium source and beak-conditioning tool.

Key nutritional concerns: Vitamin A deficiency is the most common problem in cockatiels. Signs include respiratory problems, eye discharge, and softening of the bones around the beak and choanal papillae. Dark orange and green vegetables prevent this.

African Grey Parrots

African Greys have the highest calcium requirements of any commonly kept parrot species, due to their extraordinary susceptibility to hypocalcaemia (low blood calcium), which causes seizures.

Diet: High-quality pellets should form 60–70% of the diet. Calcium-rich foods — dark leafy greens, broccoli, almonds, and kale — are important supplements. Fresh vegetables should be offered daily. A balanced supplemental diet with calcium is essential.

Avoid entirely: Sunflower seeds (highly addictive and nutritionally poor), avocado, all members of the onion family, chocolate, and caffeine.

Macaws and Large Parrots

Large parrots have higher caloric and fat requirements than smaller species. Their diet should include:

  • High-quality large parrot pellets as the foundation
  • A wide variety of fresh vegetables and fruits daily
  • Nuts (almonds, walnuts, Brazil nuts) in moderation — 2–3 per day
  • Cooked legumes and grains occasionally
  • Fresh corn, sweet potato, and leafy greens regularly

Converting a Seed-Addicted Bird to Pellets

Seed-addicted birds resist dietary change. Transition gradually:

  • Mix 10% pellets with 90% seeds initially
  • Very slowly increase pellet proportion over 4–6 weeks
  • Never remove seeds abruptly — birds will starve rather than eat an unfamiliar food
  • Offer pellets in the morning when the bird is hungriest
  • Never place pellets in the seed dish — birds learn to throw them out

Fresh Water

Change drinking water daily. Many birds dip food in water, contaminating it quickly. Water bowls should be cleaned with diluted vinegar weekly to prevent biofilm buildup.

Call 077569 65169 to book a bird nutritional consultation at Shivraj Pet Clinic, Pimple Nilakh. We treat budgies, cockatiels, African greys, lovebirds, macaws, finches, and all pet bird species.

diet-guide-budgies-cockatiels-parrots.title: Complete Diet Guide for Budgies, Cockatiels & Parrots
diet-guide-budgies-cockatiels-parrots.summary: Learn the ideal diet for budgies, cockatiels, African grey parrots, and macaws — including pellets, vegetables, fruits, and what to avoid completely.
diet-guide-budgies-cockatiels-parrots.content:
Why Diet is Everything for Pet Birds

70% of illness we see in birds at Shivraj Pet Clinic is directly linked to poor diet. Most Indian pet owners feed their birds only seeds. Seeds are high in fat and low in almost every vital nutrient.

💡 The ideal bird diet: 60–70% pellets, 20–30% fresh vegetables, and only 5–10% seeds as treats.

Budgies

  • Pellets: Harrison's Bird Foods or Zupreem — form the base of the diet
  • Vegetables: Leafy greens, carrots, capsicum
  • Fruits: Apple (no seeds), mango, pomegranate — small amounts
  • Avoid: Avocado (toxic), onion, garlic, chocolate, caffeine

Cockatiels

  • Pellet-first approach — rotate vegetables daily
  • Cooked egg — excellent protein source
  • Calcium: cuttlebone always available in cage

African Grey Parrots & Macaws

  • Cooked legumes — lentils, chickpeas (excellent protein)
  • Fresh sprouts — nutritional powerhouse
  • Macaws: include nuts in moderation — Brazil nuts, almonds
⚠️ Never feed birds: Avocado, chocolate, onion, garlic, alcohol, caffeine, fruit seeds, or any processed human food. These can be fatal even in small quantities.

sick-bird-syndrome-pet-birds.title: Sick Bird Syndrome — Early Signs Every Bird Owner Should Know
sick-bird-syndrome-pet-birds.summary: Birds hide illness until they are critically sick. Learn to recognise the early signs of a sick bird — and why acting within hours matters. From Dr. Pritesh Vidhate, Shivraj Pet Clinic Pune.
sick-bird-syndrome-pet-birds.content:
Birds are prey animals with a powerful instinct to hide illness. In the wild, showing weakness attracts predators — so birds mask signs of disease until they can no longer compensate. By the time a bird looks obviously sick, it has often been ill for days. This is why bird owners must know the subtle early signs of illness, and why acting within hours is often the difference between recovery and death.

Why Bird Illness is Deceptive

A bird that appears healthy in the morning can be critically ill by evening. Their small body mass means they have very little reserve — dehydration, hypoglycaemia, and respiratory failure can develop within hours once compensation fails. Never wait to see if a sick bird "gets better on its own."

Early Signs Your Bird is Unwell

Posture:

  • Sitting on the bottom of the cage rather than on perches
  • Fluffed up feathers — the most recognisable sign of illness in birds
  • Head tucked under the wing when not sleeping
  • Leaning to one side or having difficulty balancing

Eyes:

  • Partially closed or squinted eyes during the day
  • Discharge around the eyes
  • Sunken eyes indicate significant dehydration

Breathing:

  • Tail bobbing with each breath — indicates respiratory distress
  • Open-mouth breathing or clicking sounds
  • Voice changes or loss of vocalisation in normally vocal birds

Droppings:

  • Normal bird droppings have three components: dark green/brown faeces, white urates, and clear liquid urine
  • Abnormal: all green (bile), blood-tinged, completely liquid, black/tarry, or significantly decreased in number

Feeding and Behaviour:

  • Reduced appetite or interest in food
  • Drinking significantly more or less water than usual
  • Uncharacteristic quietness in normally active birds
  • Loss of interest in toys, social interaction, or familiar activities

Common Diseases in Pet Birds

Avian polyomavirus — affects young birds, sudden death or feather abnormalities.

Psittacine beak and feather disease (PBFD) — progressive feather destruction and beak abnormalities in parrots.

Aspergillosis — fungal respiratory infection, common in immunocompromised birds or those kept in damp environments.

Egg binding — female birds unable to pass an egg. Emergency requiring immediate veterinary care.

Crop infection (candidiasis) — yeast infection of the crop causing regurgitation and sour smell.

Proventricular dilatation disease (PDD) — progressive neurological and gastrointestinal disease in parrots.

What to Do When Your Bird is Sick

  1. Keep your bird warm — place the cage in a warm (28–30°C), quiet location. Heat support is critical as sick birds lose body temperature rapidly.
  2. Do not offer new foods or supplements — stick to familiar foods that the bird is comfortable eating.
  3. Call and come immediately — do not wait for the "next available appointment." A sick bird needs same-day care.
  4. Transport carefully — minimise stress during transport. Cover most of the carrier to create a dark, calm environment.

At Shivraj Pet Clinic, we have experience with birds of all species — from budgies and cockatiels to parrots, finches, and lovebirds. Call 077569 65169 immediately if your bird shows any of the above signs.

neutering-rabbits.title: Neutering in Rabbits — Why It Matters & How We Do It at Shivraj Pet Clinic
neutering-rabbits.summary: Rabbit neutering prevents uterine cancer and increases lifespan significantly. At Shivraj Pet Clinic we use specialised gas anaesthesia designed for small exotic animals.
neutering-rabbits.content:
Why Neutering Your Rabbit Matters

Uterine adenocarcinoma affects over 80% of unspayed female rabbits by age 5. It is the single most common cause of death in female rabbits.

🏥 At Shivraj Pet Clinic, we perform rabbit neutering using specialised exotic animal gas anaesthesia — one of very few clinics in PCMC qualified to do this safely.

Benefits of Neutering

  • Prevents uterine cancer — eliminates the #1 cause of death in females
  • Reduces territorial aggression — biting, lunging and spraying decrease significantly
  • Longer lifespan — 8–12 years vs 5–6 unneutered
  • Better bonding — calmer, more affectionate, easier to litter-train

The Procedure

  1. Pre-surgery blood test in our in-house lab
  2. Gas anaesthesia — gentle, monitored throughout
  3. Surgery: 20–35 minutes
  4. Recovery monitoring — closely watched
  5. Same-day discharge with written post-op care instructions
⚠️ Critical: Rabbits must NOT be fasted before surgery. Their digestive system must keep moving. This is a dangerous mistake made at non-exotic clinics.

Best Age to Neuter

Females: 4–6 months. Males: 3.5–5 months. Earlier is always better — cancer risk in females begins at 18 months.
